Dear Mr. Coats: 
Re: 
Request for Opinion 
No. 76-062 
1 
This is in response to your letter to Mr. Spurlock date~ 
April 20 
regarding a possible conflict of interest of a person on 
the City Council and Redevelopment Agency. 
'rhe Commission will 
not render a formal opinion on this matter at this time because 
it presents no substantial question of interpretation under the 
Po ,i t_ical Reform Act. 
Hmvever, I trust the follm"ing informal 
ad 
ce will be of assistance to you. 
The Corruuis s ion has re cen tly adopted regulations in the 
area of conflict of interest which I believe \'/ill, gi ven suffi-
cient factual development, prove dispositive of the issues you 
raise in your letter. 
The regulations are found at 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code §18700, et seq., and a copy is enclosed. 
The basic conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act. provide that: 
87100. 
No public official at any level of state or 
local government shall make, participate in making or 
in any Hay attempt to use his official position to influence 
a governmental decision in ,,7hich he knmvs or has reason to 
know he has a financial interest. 
87103. 
An official has a financial interest in a deci-
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business enti ty in vlhich the public official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000); 
(b) 
Any real property in ,,'hich the public 
ficial 
·has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) i 


x~ 
~illia~ Coats 
8 1 
1976 
(c) 
Any source of inco22, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course 
of business f aggregating two hlLldred fifty dollars 
f$250) or more in value received by or promised to the 
public official within b!elve months prior to the 
time when the decision is made; or 
It would appear that Mr. Sissell's plumbing business con-
stitutes both an "investment" and a source of "income" to him as 
defined in the Political Reform Act. 
Gov. Code §§82030 and 
8203~. 
His leasehold is an interest in real property. 
§82033. 
He would, 
therefore, be barred from participating in decisions materially 
affecting his business, if the effect of the decisions is distin-
guishable from its effect on the public generally. 
However, in 
light of the facts, as you state them, it is difficult to imagine 
very many si tuations where the prohibition would apply to 
Mr. Sissell's vote. 
Mr. Sissell's contracts with and interest in 
the project area appear to be so minimal and tangential that it 
is unlikely the foreseeable effects of decisions regarding the 
p 
ect area will meet the indicia of materiality expressed in the 
regulations. 
See §18702 "Material Financial Effect." 
It also 
would appear that even if this test of materiality were met, all 
or most all of the decisions would likely have the same effect on 
fIche public generally" or a IIsignificaHt segment" of the public of 
which Mr. Sissell's business would be a part. 
See regulations, 
§18704. 
In sum, I"dhile it is clear that the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act apply to Mr. Sissell in his official capaci 
I am unable to hypothesize a situation which "lould call for his 
disqualification. 
I trust this answers your question. 
If, hmvever, you wish 
to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so pur-
suant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18321. 
If you have any further ques-
tions concerning this matter, please feel free to call upon 
Delbert Spurlock I Chief of the Com.Tfliss ion's Conflict of Interest 
Division, at (916) 322-6441. 
,Vti3: pvp 
Enclosure 
Sincerely r 
Michael Bennett 
Executi ve Director 
1

Fe ir Poli t ieill Prilet ices CO;-C'~l:SS lO. 
Post Office Box 807 
S~erilmento, California 95804

~l..:.... -L Gl't • 
Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief 
Conflicts of Interest Division 
Dear Mr. Spurlock: 
20, 1 
I am the City Attorney of the City of Tracy. 
I spoke with you in 
the middle of March regarding a question of conflict of interest 
involving one of our :lewly elected City Councilmen. 
~;:e would appre:" 
ciate an advisory opinion from the Commission pursuant to §83114 of 
the Government Code of the councilman's duty to disclose and/or ab-
stain from voting on the following facts: 
The City Council of the City of Tracy created a redevelo?ment agency 
and declared itself to be the agency. .The proposed redevelopment 
plan includes the redevelopment of a portion of the central business 
district. 
The entire area to be redeveloped was classified as the 
project area. 
The project area was divided into four sub-areas. 
The redevelopment plan calls for demolition and reconstruction ill 
Sub-area 1, limited demolition and construction of parking in Sub-
area 2, and would rely upon owner restoration in Sub-areas 3 and 4. 
Councilman Roy Sissell conducts a plumbing business from a leased 
building located in Sub-area 3. 
His building is one full block 
from the boundary of Sub-area 1. 
Mr. Sissell has a one year's 
lease on the property. 
He does very little retail sales from the 
building. 
He uses the area as his headquarters and for storage of 
parts and supplies to conduct his business throughout the community. 
He has no obligation to restore the property, or otherwise maintain 
it, except for damages caused by his use and occupancy_ 
Under these facts, would Mr. Sissell be disqualified from voting O:l 
issues coming before the Redevelopment Agency and before the Council 
which involved the Redevelopment Agency? 
Very truly yours, 
HC/ps

sections.question: Under these facts, would Mr. Sissell be disqualified from voting on issues coming before the Redevelopment Agency and before the Council which involved the Redevelopment Agency?
sections.conclusion: In sum, while it is clear that the provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to Mr. Sissell in his official capacity, I am unable to hypothesize a situation which would call for his disqualification.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether City Councilman Roy Sissell, who operates a plumbing business from a leased building in Sub-area 3 of a redevelopment project area, is disqualified from voting on redevelopment agency matters due to conflict of interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: While the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions technically apply to Mr. Sissell's business interests, his contracts and interests in the project area are so minimal and tangential that disqualification would be unlikely, and most decisions would likely have the same effect on the public generally or a significant segment thereof.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated April 20, 1976) and FPPC's response letter (dated May 18, 1976). Response is informal advice rather than formal opinion. Specific person named (Roy Sissell) and factual scenario provided. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers present but sections are clearly delineated.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under these facts, would Mr. Sissell be disqualified from voting on issues coming before the Redevelopment Agency and before the Council which involved the Redevelopment Agency?

CONCLUSION: In sum, while it is clear that the provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to Mr. Sissell in his official capacity, I am unable to hypothesize a situation which would call for his disqualification.

embedding.summary: FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion but provided informal advice on whether City Councilman Roy Sissell must disqualify himself from voting on redevelopment agency matters affecting the area where his plumbing business is located. The FPPC concluded that while conflict of interest provisions apply, Sissell's minimal involvement makes disqualification unlikely.
